params.description: Sticker sheet appears showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in a clear rectangular arrangement.

beats.text: In the Warmup, you saw equal groups lined up into a rectangle. Check out this sticker sheet. The stickers aren't bunched up or scattered around. They're lined up. Every sticker has its own spot. Here's what I notice.

elements.description: Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in a clear rectangular arrangement.

beats.text: See how they're lined up? They go across, and they go up and down. Everything lines up into a rectangle.

params.description: System briefly outlines the full rectangle shape around the sticker sheet.

elements.description: Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers. Rectangle outline visible around the array.

beats.text: When objects are arranged like this, lined up in a rectangle with every spot filled, that's called an array.

elements.description: Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in rectangular arrangement.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4, counting columns instead of rows

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially, left to right.

steps.text: You counted columns. Columns go up and down. Rows go across. Count the lines going across.

incorrects.description: Student selected 7, adding rows and columns together

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially, left to right.

steps.text: The question asks for rows only, not the total. Rows are the lines going across. Count just the rows.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2, miscounting the rows

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially, left to right.

steps.text: Let's count together. Rows go across, left to right. Watch each row light up as we count.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All 3 rows highlight one at a time, with labels: row 1, row 2, row 3.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the lines going across. One, two, three. Row 1, row 2, row 3. The answer is 3. Rows go across, and this array has 3 rows.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6: counted columns instead of rows

params.description: The 2 horizontal rows highlight.

steps.text: You counted the columns. Rows go across, like a line you'd read. How many lines go across?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8: likely added rows and columns

params.description: The 2 horizontal rows highlight.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We're only counting rows right now. Rows go across. Count just the lines going across.

incorrects.description: Student selected 12: counted total dots

params.description: The 2 horizontal rows highlight.

steps.text: You counted the dots. We need to count the rows. A row is one line going across. How many lines go across?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System counts each row: first row across, then second row across.

steps.text: Let me show you how to count rows. I start at the top. First row across. Then the second row across. That's 2 rows total. Rows are the lines going across.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2: counted rows instead of columns

params.description: The 6 vertical columns highlight.

steps.text: You counted the rows. Columns go up and down, not across. How many lines go up and down?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8: likely added rows and columns

params.description: The 6 vertical columns highlight.

steps.text: Here's a hint: we're only counting columns now. Columns go up and down. Count just the lines going up and down.

incorrects.description: Student selected 12: counted total dots

params.description: The 6 vertical columns highlight.

steps.text: You counted the dots. We need to count the columns. A column is one line going up and down. How many lines go up and down?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System counts each column from left to right: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. I start on the left. I count each line going up and down: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. That's 6 columns. Columns are the lines going up and down.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows. That's your first number.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Rows highlight sequentially: row 1, row 2, row 3, row 4. Each row has 2 dots.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the rows: that's 4. Count the dots in one row: that's 2. First number is rows, second is items per row.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Rows highlight sequentially with count: 1, 2, 3, 4 rows.
4 tile places into first slot.
2 tile places into second slot. Expression shows 4 × 2.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the rows: 1, 2, 3, 4. That's the first number. I count dots in one row: 2. That's the second number. The expression is 4 times 2. That's 4 rows of 2.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the array. How many rows do you see?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: Each row pulses to highlight the row structure.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the rows: 2 rows. Count the dots in one row: 5 dots. So it's 2 times 5.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: First row highlights, then second row highlights, showing 2 rows total.
System places 2 in first slot, then 5 in second slot.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the rows: 2 rows. I count how many dots are in each row: 5 dots. So the expression is 2 times 5. That tells us there are 2 rows of 5.

params.description: 2×5 dot array appears on screen. 2 rows, 5 columns of dots.

beats.text: You've been describing arrays by rows, those lines going across. But here's the thing about arrays. There's another way to describe them.

params.description: Rows briefly highlight then fade.
Columns brighten and highlight.

elements.description: 2×5 dot array. Columns highlighted.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the columns going up and down first.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers on second attempt

params.description: Columns highlight one at a time: 1, 2, 3.

steps.text: Let's count together. I see 3 columns going up and down. Each column has 4 dots in it.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect numbers three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Columns highlight sequentially, then system places tiles.
System places 3 and 4 in the word description slots.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the columns: 1, 2, 3. That's 3 columns. Then I count the dots in one column: 1, 2, 3, 4. So this is 3 columns of 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. Look at the word description above. What does it say?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Word description 3 columns of 4 highlights.

steps.text: Here's what helps: 3 columns of 4 means the first number is 3 and the second number is 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places tiles in order: 3, then ×, then 4.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this. 3 columns of 4 becomes 3 times 4. The first number in my description becomes the first number in my expression. The expression is 3 times 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows going across. How many rows are there?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Rows highlight on the array.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. I see 4 rows going across. Each row has 5 dots. That makes 4 times 5.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Rows highlight on the array.
System places 4 × 5 in the equation builder.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the rows going across. I see 4 rows. Then I count how many dots are in each row. That's 5. So the expression is 4 times 5. 4 rows with 5 in each row.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. Count the columns going up and down. How many columns do you see?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression on second attempt

params.description: Columns pulse on the array.

steps.text: Here's a hint. I see 5 columns standing up. Each column has 4 dots. That gives us 5 times 4.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect expression three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Columns highlight on the array.
System places 5 × 4 in the equation builder.

steps.text: Let me walk you through this one. I count the columns standing up and down. I see 5 columns. Then I count how many dots are in each column. That's 4. So the expression is 5 times 4. 5 columns with 4 in each column.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows, then count how many are in each row.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: Rows highlight on dot array.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. I see 2 rows. Each row has 6 dots. So the row expression is 2 times 6.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Rows highlight on dot array.
System places tiles 2 and 6. Expression shows 2 × 6.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I count the rows. That's 2. Then I count the dots in one row. That's 6. The row expression is 2 times 6.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt (rows path)

steps.text: Not quite. Count the rows going across. How many rows do you see?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt (rows path)

params.description: All three rows highlight in sequence.

steps.text: Let's count together. There are 3 rows. Each row has 4 items. So we write 3 rows of 4 items, which is 3 times 4 equals 12.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places tiles: 3 in first slot, 4 in second slot.
System places tiles: 3, 4, 12 in equation slots.

steps.text: Here's how to describe this array by rows. I count 3 rows going across. Each row has 4 items. That gives us 3 rows of 4 items. In numbers, that's 3 times 4 equals 12. The answer is 3 rows of 4, or 3 times 4 equals 12.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on first attempt (columns path)

steps.text: Check the columns going up and down. How many columns are there?

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values on second attempt (columns path)

params.description: All four columns highlight in sequence.

steps.text: Here's what helps. There are 4 columns. Each column has 3 items. So it's 4 columns of 3 items, which is 4 times 3 equals 12.

incorrects.description: Student placed incorrect values three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places tiles: 4 in first slot, 3 in second slot.
System places tiles: 4, 3, 12 in equation slots.

steps.text: Let me walk you through this one. I count 4 columns going up and down. Each column has 3 items. That means 4 columns of 3 items. In numbers, 4 times 3 equals 12. The answer is 4 columns of 3, or 4 times 3 equals 12.

params.description: Equation builder appears in word specification template format.

beats.text: You can describe the same array two ways -- by rows or by columns. Each way gives you a different expression. Now let's flip it. You're going to BUILD arrays. You tell the computer what to make, then find the answer.

elements.description: Equation builder in word specification template format.

params.description: Empty array build area. No rows or items visible yet.
Row builder panel appears at bottom. Row +/- buttons active. Items per Row +/- buttons dimmed.

beats.text: Now you get to build arrays. Let me show you how this works.

elements.description: Empty array build area.
Row builder panel. Row +/- active, Items per Row +/- dimmed.

beats.text: I want to make 3 rows of 5. I said rows first, so I'll build the rows first.

elements.description: Empty array build area.
Row builder panel. Row +/- active, Items per Row +/- dimmed.

params.description: Row + button highlights.
First empty row placeholder appears, a faint horizontal line indicating where the row will be. No dots yet.

elements.description: Array build area showing 1 empty row placeholder, no dots.
Row builder panel. Row +/- active, Items per Row +/- dimmed.

params.description: Second empty row placeholder appears below the first.

elements.description: Array build area showing 2 empty row placeholders, no dots.
Row builder panel. Row +/- active, Items per Row +/- dimmed.

beats.text: Third row. Three rows set up, but no dots yet. I need to add columns to fill them in.

params.description: Third empty row placeholder appears.
Row +/- buttons dim and disable. Items per Row +/- buttons activate.

elements.description: Array build area showing 3 empty row placeholders, no dots.
Row builder panel. Row +/- dimmed, Items per Row +/- active.

beats.text: Now I add items to each row. Watch this. Each plus puts one dot in every row.

params.description: Items per Row + button highlights.
First column appears: one dot in each of the 3 rows, forming a vertical line at the left. 3 dots total.

elements.description: Array build area showing 3 rows with 1 dot each. 3 dots total.
Row builder panel. Row +/- dimmed, Items per Row +/- active.

beats.text: One dot in each row. That's 3 dots total.

params.description: Second column appears. 2 dots in each row now. 6 dots total.

elements.description: Array build area showing 3 rows with 2 dots each. 6 dots total.
Row builder panel. Row +/- dimmed, Items per Row +/- active.

beats.text: Two dots in each row. Six dots. Keep watching.

params.description: Third column appears. 3 dots in each row. 9 dots total.
Fourth column appears. 4 dots in each row. 12 dots total.
Fifth column appears. 5 dots in each row. 15 dots total.

elements.description: Array build area showing complete 3 by 5 array. 3 rows with 5 dots each. 15 dots total.
Row builder panel. Row +/- dimmed, Items per Row +/- active.

beats.text: Three, four, five in each row. Three rows of 5. Fifteen dots. Done.

params.description: Items per Row +/- buttons dim and disable.

elements.description: Complete 3 by 5 array. 3 rows with 5 dots each. 15 dots total.
Row builder panel. Both Row +/- and Items per Row +/- dimmed.

params.description: Equation builder appears in equation mode. Template shows blank times blank equals blank. Tile palette displays numbers 3, 4, 5, 8, 12, 15, 18, 20.
Guide places tiles 3, 5, 15 into the three slots in sequence.

elements.description: Complete 3 by 5 array. 3 rows with 5 dots each. 15 dots total.
Row builder panel. Both buttons dimmed.
Equation builder showing 3 x 5 = 15. All slots filled.

beats.text: Three rows of 5. Three times 5 equals 15. I planned it, built it, got my answer. Your turn now.

elements.description: Complete 3 by 5 array. 3 rows with 5 dots each. 15 dots total.
Row builder panel. Both buttons dimmed.
Equation builder showing 3 x 5 = 15. All slots filled.

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of rows on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count how many rows you need.

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of rows on second attempt

params.description: Row button pulses to draw attention.

steps.text: You're working on it. The problem says 4 rows. Keep pressing the Row + button until you see 4 empty rows.

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of rows three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System adds rows one at a time until 4 empty row placeholders are visible.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I press the Row + button 4 times. That gives me 4 empty rows. The answer is 4 rows.

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of items per row on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. How many dots should go in each row?

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of items per row on second attempt

params.description: Items per Row button pulses to draw attention.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. The problem says 4 rows of 6. That means 6 dots in every row. Keep pressing Items per Row + until each row has 6 dots.

incorrects.description: Student added wrong number of items per row three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System adds dots one at a time to each row until every row has 6 dots. 24 dots total visible.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. I press Items per Row + 6 times. Now every row has 6 dots. The answer is 6 items per row.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers in equation on first attempt

steps.text: Let's try again. Look at the array you built.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers in equation on second attempt

params.description: Array pulses. Rows and items per row highlight briefly.

steps.text: Here's a hint: count the rows going down. That's your first number. Count the dots across one row. That's your second number. Multiply to find how many dots total.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong numbers in equation three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places tiles: 4 in first blank, 6 in second blank, 24 in third blank. Equation reads 4 x 6 = 24.

steps.text: Let me show you how to think about this. The array has 4 rows. That's the first number. Each row has 6 dots. That's the second number. 4 times 6 equals 24. The equation is 4 x 6 = 24.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Column + wrong number of times on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. We need 2 columns. Check how many you added.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Column + wrong number of times on second attempt

params.description: Column counter pulses to highlight current value.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. You need exactly 2 columns. Look at the counter and press + until it shows 2.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Column + wrong number of times three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System presses Column + twice, counter changes 0 to 1 to 2.
2 empty column placeholders appear.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I press Column + two times. Now we have 2 empty columns. The answer is 2 columns.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Items per Column + wrong number of times on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. We need 5 dots in each column. Count how many you added.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Items per Column + wrong number of times on second attempt

params.description: Items per Column counter pulses to highlight current value.

steps.text: You're working on it. Here's what helps: each press adds 1 dot to every column. Keep pressing until each column has 5 dots total.

incorrects.description: Student pressed Items per Column + wrong number of times three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System presses Items per Column + five times. Counter changes 0 to 1 to 2 to 3 to 4 to 5. Dots fill into both columns one at a time.
Complete 5×2 dot array visible. Each column has 5 dots, 10 dots total.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. I press Items per Column + five times. Each press puts 1 dot in every column. After 5 presses, each column has 5 dots. The answer is 5 dots per column.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong equation on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look at this problem again. You built 2 columns. Each column has 5 dots.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong equation on second attempt

params.description: The 2 columns highlight briefly, then the 5 dots in each column highlight.

steps.text: Here's a hint: count the columns first. That's your first number. Then count how many dots are in one column. That's your second number.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong equation three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System places tiles: 2, then ×, then 5, then =, then 10. Each tile appears in sequence.
As equation builds, 2 columns highlight, then 5 dots in each column highlight, then all 10 dots highlight.

steps.text: Let me show you how to think about this. First, I count the columns: 2. Then I count the dots in one column: 5. The equation is 2 times 5 equals 10. That matches what we built.

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count all the dots. Does that equal 20?

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total on second attempt

params.description: All dots in array pulse to highlight the total count.

steps.text: Let's think about this together. You need exactly 20 dots total. Count your array. Do you have too many or too few?

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total three or more times. System models a valid solution.

params.description: System demonstrates building a 4 by 5 array with 20 dots.

steps.text: Let me show you one way. I'll build 4 rows with 5 dots in each row. 4 times 5 equals 20. That gives me exactly 20 dots. You could also do 5 rows of 4, or 2 rows of 10, or other combinations.

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total on first attempt

steps.text: Check the total. Count all the dots. Does that equal 20?

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total on second attempt

params.description: All dots in array pulse to highlight the total count.

steps.text: Here's a hint: you need exactly 20 dots in your array. Count what you have now. Adjust the columns or items until the total is 20.

incorrects.description: Student built array with wrong total three or more times. System models a valid solution.

params.description: System demonstrates building a 5 by 4 array with 20 dots.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. I'll make 5 columns with 4 dots in each column. 5 times 4 equals 20. That's exactly 20 dots. You could also build it other ways, like 4 columns of 5 or 10 columns of 2.

incorrects.description: Student placed word description that does not match their array on first attempt

steps.text: Look at your array. Count the groups and count how many are in each group.

incorrects.description: Student placed word description that does not match their array on second attempt

params.description: Array rows or columns highlight to show the grouping structure.

steps.text: You're working on it. Look closely at your array. If you built rows, count the rows. Then count how many dots are in each row. Use those numbers.

incorrects.description: Student placed word description that does not match their array. System models the correct description.

params.description: System places the correct word description matching the student's array.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. Your array shows the groups and items you built. If you made 4 rows with 5 in each row, the description is 4 groups of 5. That matches your array and gives 20 total.

incorrects.description: Student placed equation that does not match their array on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at the word description you wrote. The equation uses those same numbers.

incorrects.description: Student placed equation that does not match their array on second attempt

params.description: Word description highlights to draw attention to the numbers.

steps.text: Let's think about this a bit more. Your word description tells you the equation. If you wrote 4 groups of 5, the equation is 4 times 5 equals 20.

incorrects.description: Student placed equation that does not match their array. System models the correct equation.

params.description: System places the correct equation matching the student's array and word description.

steps.text: Let me help a bit more. Your array and word description show what the equation should be. If you built 4 rows of 5, that's 4 times 5 equals 20. The equation matches the array you made.

beats.text: You can describe arrays by rows or columns now. You can write expressions for both. And you can find the product. Let's see how you do with this.
